tasks.title: Branch gate: feature/per-aircraft-ci
tasks.description: Create and switch to a new feature branch from the current main/development branch.
tasks.details:
Run:
  git checkout -b feature/per-aircraft-ci
  git branch --show-current

Confirm output is `feature/per-aircraft-ci`. Do NOT proceed to any other task until this is verified.
tasks.testStrategy: Run `git branch --show-current` and confirm output is `feature/per-aircraft-ci`. Run `git status` and confirm working tree is clean.

tasks.title: Add ci column to my_fleet with migration
tasks.description: Add a `ci` INTEGER column (default 200) to the `my_fleet` table. This is schema + migration only — no UI changes yet.
tasks.details:
Files touched (max 3):

1. `database/schema.py` — Two changes:
   a. Update the canonical `my_fleet` CREATE TABLE DDL to include `ci INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 200 CHECK (ci >= 0 AND ci <= 200)` after the `quantity` column.
   b. Add an idempotent migration function (following the existing `_migrate_*` pattern): check `PRAGMA table_info(my_fleet)` for the `ci` column; if missing, `ALTER TABLE my_fleet ADD COLUMN ci INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 200`. Do NOT add a CHECK constraint via ALTER (SQLite doesn't support it) — the CHECK goes only in the canonical CREATE TABLE for fresh DBs. Register the migration call in the existing migration runner so it runs at startup.

2. `database/fleet_dao.py` (or wherever my_fleet CRUD lives) — Update any INSERT/UPDATE functions to accept and persist `ci`. Update SELECT queries that return fleet rows to include `ci` in the result set. The default for new inserts where ci is not specified should be 200.

3. `tests/test_fleet_dao.py` (or equivalent) — Add tests: (a) fresh DB has ci column with default 200, (b) inserting a fleet entry with ci=150 persists correctly, (c) inserting without specifying ci defaults to 200, (d) ci=201 or ci=-1 is rejected on fresh DBs (CHECK constraint), (e) migration on existing DB adds the column with default 200 for all existing rows.

IMPORTANT: The `route_aircraft` table already has a `ci` column storing the CI used during extraction. That column is unrelated to this change — do NOT modify `route_aircraft` in this task. The new `my_fleet.ci` column stores the player's *desired* CI per aircraft type, which will later be fed into extraction.
tasks.testStrategy: Run pytest on the fleet DAO tests — all pass. Start the server against an existing DB with fleet entries, then query: `SELECT aircraft_id, ci FROM my_fleet LIMIT 5` — all existing rows should show ci=200. Insert a new fleet entry via the existing UI/API and confirm ci=200 is stored. Manually UPDATE one row to ci=150, confirm it persists across server restart.

tasks.title: Expose CI as editable field in My Fleet UI
tasks.description: Add an inline-editable CI column to the My Fleet page so users can set CI per aircraft type. Default display is 200. Saves via HTMX without full page reload.
tasks.details:
Files touched (max 3):

1. `dashboard/templates/my_fleet.html` (or the relevant partial) — Add a `CI` column to the fleet table. Render as a number input (`type=number`, min=0, max=200, step=1) inside each row. Wire it with `hx-post` or `hx-patch` to save on change/blur, targeting just the row or a confirmation indicator. Show the current `ci` value from the DB.

2. `dashboard/routes/pages.py` or `dashboard/routes/api_routes.py` (whichever serves the fleet API) — Add an endpoint `PATCH /api/fleet/{aircraft_id}/ci` (or fold into an existing fleet update endpoint) that accepts `{"ci": int}`, validates 0-200 range, calls the DAO to update, and returns an HTMX fragment confirming the save (e.g. a brief flash or the updated cell).

3. `dashboard/routes/pages.py` (if the GET /my-fleet handler needs updating) — Ensure the fleet query includes `ci` so the template can render it. If the handler already returns full fleet rows from the DAO, this may be a no-op.

UX notes:
- The CI input should look like other editable fields in the fleet table (match existing styling)
- A subtle save indicator (checkmark flash, green border pulse) on successful HTMX response
- If the user types a value outside 0-200, show a client-side validation error (HTML5 min/max handles this)
- Consider a "Set all to..." bulk action button above the table that sets every aircraft's CI to a user-entered value (optional, nice-to-have)
tasks.testStrategy: Load /my-fleet — CI column appears with 200 for all aircraft. Change one aircraft's CI to 150, confirm the save indicator fires and the value persists on page reload. Try entering 201 — browser validation blocks it. Try entering -1 — blocked. Open dev tools network tab, confirm the PATCH request fires and returns 200.

tasks.title: Thread per-aircraft CI through extraction
tasks.description: Modify the route extraction to look up each aircraft's CI from my_fleet instead of using the global hardcoded CI=200. Routes for aircraft not in my_fleet still default to 200.
tasks.details:
Files touched (max 3):

1. `extractors/routes.py` (or equivalent extraction module) — This is the critical change. Currently the extraction builds a `UserConfig` with a single `ci` value (200) and passes it to every `AircraftRoute` / `RoutesSearch` call. Change this to:
   a. At the start of extraction, query `my_fleet` to build a dict: `fleet_ci = {aircraft_id: ci for aircraft_id, ci in conn.execute('SELECT aircraft_id, ci FROM my_fleet')}`
   b. In the per-aircraft loop, look up `ci = fleet_ci.get(current_aircraft_id, 200)` and pass that CI to the am4 package call for this specific aircraft.
   c. The `ci` column in `route_aircraft` already stores the CI used — it will now correctly reflect per-aircraft values instead of a flat 200.

   IMPORTANT: Understand how the am4 package accepts CI. Check whether it's on the `User` object, passed to `AircraftRoute`, or a parameter on `RoutesSearch`. Grep for `ci` and `cost_index` in the am4 package imports and usage. The CI must be set per-aircraft-iteration, not once globally.

2. `dashboard/routes/api_routes.py` (or wherever hub refresh triggers extraction) — If the dashboard's hub refresh endpoint constructs a `UserConfig` and passes CI, update it to either omit the global CI (since extraction now looks it up per-aircraft) or pass it as a fallback default. Do NOT break the existing hub refresh flow.

3. `config.py` (if it exists and hardcodes `ci=200`) — Either remove the hardcoded default, mark it as a fallback-only value with a comment explaining that `my_fleet.ci` takes precedence, or leave it as the default for aircraft not in my_fleet. Do NOT remove the CLI `--ci` flag if it exists — keep it as the fallback for aircraft without a fleet entry.

Edge cases:
- Aircraft in route_aircraft but NOT in my_fleet: use fallback CI (200 or CLI value)
- my_fleet entry with ci=0: valid, means slowest speed — do not skip or error
- Extraction running while user edits CI on /my-fleet: fine, extraction reads CI at start and uses that snapshot
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Set one aircraft in my_fleet to ci=100 via the UI (task 3). 2. Trigger a hub refresh for a hub that uses that aircraft. 3. After extraction completes, query: `SELECT aircraft_id, ci, contribution FROM route_aircraft WHERE aircraft_id = <that_aircraft> LIMIT 5` — ci should be 100, and contribution should differ from the ci=200 routes. 4. Check another aircraft that was left at ci=200 — its routes should still show ci=200. 5. Confirm the Contribution Optimizer page now shows varying contribution values for aircraft with different CIs.

tasks.title: Format contribution display with decimals and update C/P ratio
tasks.description: Update the Contribution Optimizer page to show contribution with 2 decimal places instead of whole dollars, and fix C/P ratio to show meaningful precision.
tasks.details:
Files touched (max 2):

1. `dashboard/templates/contributions.html` (or the partial that renders the contributions table) — Change the contribution column format from `$XX` to `$XX.XX` (2 decimal places). Change the C/P ratio column from 3 decimal places to 6 decimal places (or scientific notation) since contribution is ~$83 and profit is ~$2M, making the ratio ~0.00004. Alternatively, display C/P as "contribution per $1000 profit" to get more human-readable numbers.

2. `dashboard/routes/pages.py` or `dashboard/routes/api_routes.py` (if formatting happens server-side via Jinja filters or Python formatting) — Update the number formatting for contribution and C/P ratio columns. If there's a shared currency formatter being used, check whether it's truncating to whole dollars and fix it there (but be careful not to break other pages where whole-dollar display is correct, like Profit/d).

Consider: The Profit/d column showing $2,043,692 as whole dollars is fine — those are large numbers where cents don't matter. But $83 contribution losing its decimals is the exact problem. The fix should be targeted to contribution and C/P, not a global change.
tasks.testStrategy: Load /contributions — contribution column shows values like $83.47, $83.37 etc. (not flat $83). C/P ratio shows non-zero values with enough precision to differentiate routes. Sort by contribution — ordering should now visibly differ between routes. Sort by C/P ratio — should produce a meaningful ranking.

tasks.title: Verification and merge prep
tasks.description: End-to-end verification that per-aircraft CI works correctly across the full flow, then prepare for merge.
tasks.details:
Steps:

1. Fresh-DB smoke: delete the DB, start the server, confirm my_fleet table has ci column, add an aircraft — ci defaults to 200.

2. Per-aircraft CI flow: set aircraft A to ci=100, aircraft B to ci=200. Run extraction for a hub that covers both. Query route_aircraft and confirm:
   - Aircraft A routes have ci=100
   - Aircraft B routes have ci=200
   - Contribution values differ between A and B for the same destination

3. UI verification: load /my-fleet, confirm CI column is editable. Load /contributions, confirm varying contributions with decimal display. C/P ratio is non-zero.

4. Run full test suite: `pytest tests/ -v` — all green, count >= 174.

5. Run `git diff feature/per-aircraft-ci --stat` and scan for unintended changes.

6. Push branch: `git push -u origin feature/per-aircraft-ci`

7. Surface to user: report pass/fail of every step, include before/after contribution values for the two test aircraft as proof. Recommend merge or further work.

Do NOT auto-merge. Surface and wait for user direction.
tasks.testStrategy: All 7 steps above must pass. The verification report must include concrete contribution numbers for two aircraft with different CIs on the same route, demonstrating that per-aircraft CI produces different contribution values.

metadata.description: Add per-aircraft CI (Cost Index) to my_fleet, replacing the global hardcoded CI=200. Each aircraft type gets its own CI (default 200) stored in my_fleet, editable via the My Fleet page, and threaded through extraction so route_aircraft.contribution reflects the actual CI per aircraft. Also fixes the Contribution Optimizer display to show decimal precision instead of truncated whole dollars.
